Title: Putting It All Together | Cursor Learn
URL: https://cursor.com/learn/putting-it-together
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
You now know how to use agents to understand codebases, create features quickly, search through your code and find bugs, review code more efficiently, and customize agents to your needs. This chapter shows an example of putting all of those pieces together.
...
You've joined a team that runs an ecommerce platform. You need to add discount codes to the checkout flow. The codebase is unfamiliar, and the feature needs to ship by end of week.
...
This is a realistic situation. You need to explore before you build, plan before you code, test before you ship, and review before you merge.
...
Before touching anything, explore the checkout flow. Use the agent to build a mental map.
...
This combines the grep and semantic search from understanding your codebase to trace the checkout flow, then summarizes what it finds.
...
Now that you understand the codebase, create a plan. Switch to Plan Mode (`Shift+Tab`) and outline the work:
...
Add discount code support to checkout.
...
enter a code, the system validates it
...
discount is applied to the order total before
...
Review the plan. Remove steps that are too ambitious for the first iteration. Focus on the core flow: apply a code, calculate the discount, show it in the summary. Then click Build to create the first version of this feature.
...
After the build, one test keeps failing: stacking a percentage discount with a fixed-amount discount produces the wrong total. The same set of discounts gives different results depending on the order they arrive.
...
Use Debug Mode or ask the agent to investigate the root cause:
...
The discount stacking test is failing. The same discounts produce different totals depending on application order. Find out why and fix the ordering of discount application in {{file:src/services/DiscountService.ts}}. Show me the before and after values at each step.
...
## Step 4: Review and test
...
Review all the changes I've made for the discount code feature. Check for bugs, missing error handling, edge cases, and consistency with existing patterns. What would a code reviewer flag?
...
If you have cloud agents set up, launch a few to test edge cases you might have missed: unicode characters in discount codes, large discount values, or concurrent code usage. Cloud agents report back with the cases they covered and areas that need more testing, so you can fill gaps before merging.
...
You learned something during this feature: discount calculations should always apply percentage discounts before fixed-amount discounts so results are consistent regardless of input order. Capture this in a rule so the agent knows for next time:
...
This completes the loop. You explored, planned, built, tested, debugged, reviewed, and captured knowledge for

Title: Case Study: How a Startup Built 10× Faster with Cursor 2.0 - Skywork ai
URL: https://skywork.ai/blog/vibecoding/cursor-2-0-case-study-startup/
Published: 2025-11-05T10:02:51.000Z
Author: Millie
Highlights:
Picture a tiny, punchy team: 2 full‑stack devs, 1 data/ML engineer, 1 designer who can code, and the founder who writes decent SQL (dangerous combo, I know). The product: an AI‑native SaaS that drafts sales collateral from CRM data with human-in-the-loop edits. We decided to build live inside Cursor 2.0, leaning on its multi-agent workflow and inline chat.
...
They’d tried the usual: VS Code + extensions + ChatGPT in the browser. The switching cost was real. Cursor 2.0’s pitch is “stay in the editor: let agents propose and apply code changes across files.” That mattered. The editor-level context window meant fewer copy-paste moments, and the diff UI made it easy to accept/reject suggestions. Also, multi-agent roles (frontend/backend/DB/DevOps) felt closer to how we actually work than a single omniscient assistant. If you’re curious, Cursor explains the agents-plus-diff flow on their site.
...
- Week 1: Schema design, auth, skeleton UI. Cursor agents wrote 70% of boilerplate. We iterated prompts to standardize naming conventions (I almost skipped this: glad I didn’t, huge diff later).
- Week 2: Core flows: OAuth sync, embedding service, job queue, and editor UX. We stress-tested prompts and had agents refactor cross-file types in one go. This was the “oh wow” week.
- Week 3: Hardening and polish: rate limits, error states, analytics events, and a tiny beta release to five design partners. Cursor helped rip through bug queues with quick diffs.
...
The big unlock: asking agents to propose end-to-end changes. Example: “Add draft versioning with optimistic UI.” Cursor proposed DB migration, updated API routes, React state changes, and tests. We reviewed diffs, asked for tweaks, and shipped. Ten times faster sounds like hype: it didn’t feel like hype when we closed tickets before lunch. Caveat: agents occasionally hallucinated an import path: the diff review made those easy to catch.
...
Roughly 10
...
boilerplate and refactors
...
authored tests in Week
...
three months (back-of-
...
Cursor 2.0 is an AI-powered code editor with multi-agent workflows and a diff-first UI. In a three-week sprint, it handled scaffolding, cross-file changes, and tests, letting a five-person team ship an AI-native SaaS MVP fast. Staying in-editor reduced context switching and sped up routine work dramatically.
...
Teams can define role-specific agents—Frontend, Backend, Database, and DevOps—plus a shared glossary for naming, entities, and error codes. Cursor’s cross-file awareness lets agents coordinate changes (e.g., matching Zod schemas to new form fields) and propose end-to-end updates spanning migrations, routes, React state, and tests.

Title: Cursor 2.0: A Complete Guide With Python Project | DataCamp
URL: https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/cursor-2-0-complete-guide
Published: 2025-11-11T00:00:00.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
At the end of last month, Cursor announced Cursor 2.0, a new version of Cursor that brings agentic development to Cursor.
...
In this tutorial, I’ll explore the main features of the new Cursor 2.0 by building a currency exchange web app in Python.
...
Cursor introduces its first in-house coding model named Composer. They claim it is ~4× faster than similarly capable models and is tuned specifically for agentic, multi-step code workflows. Its main goal is to reduce latency so you feel more like you’re interacting in real-time with the AI rather than waiting.
...
Cursor 2.0 shifts from one agent helping you to many agents running in parallel. Up to 8 agents can run in parallel on a single prompt. Each agent gets its own isolated copy of the codebase so they don’t conflict. A sidebar UI shows your agents/plans, and you can pick the best output from multiple runs.
...
To test Cursor 2, we created a web app for currency conversion using Python. The app is entirely created using Cursor 2.
...
The new "Agents" view organizes our changes as conversations with different AI models. The workflow is similar to using a ChatGPT conversation per feature, except it's all directly integrated inside Cursor 2.

...

...
agents to iteratively improve their output until it meets the required criteria. This combination is particularly useful for handling complex projects, as it can help speed up reviewing, testing, and coding tasks.
...
state-of-the-art AI coding models

...
input and select "Use Multiple Models".
...
Instead of having the agent directly propose code changes, we can use the “Plan” mode to first work on an implementation plan. This makes it possible to iterate on the plan and make sure it is implementing what we want before making any changes to the code.
...
The integrated multi-agent interface enables users to run several models in parallel on a single problem, visually compare their outputs, and select the most suitable solution.
...
Assisted by tools like a built-in browser for in-app web testing and an enhanced UI for organizing work across agents. These improvements collectively create a much more integrated, seamless, and intelligent development environment than previous versions could offer.
...
Cursor 2.0 adds a multi-agent system, the new Composer model, and a built-in browser for testing. The core editor is still familiar, but the workflow is more agent-driven and better suited for complex tasks.

Title: How I Use Cursor to Plan and Ship | Rachel Cantor
URL: https://rachel.fyi/posts/how-i-use-cursor-to-plan-and-ship/
Published: 2026-01-29T05:00:00.000Z
Author: Rachel Cantor
Highlights:
Use Cursor to Plan
...
## Breaking Down
...
Before writing code, I use Cursor’s planning tools to map out the architecture. The key is avoiding the “Oprah-voice” approach (Backend gets an agent! Frontend gets an agent!). Instead, I think in terms of testable deliverables.
...
1. Linear dependencies: Tasks that must be completed before the next can begin.
2. Parallel tracks: Non-blocking tasks I can delegate to a separate Composer session.
...
For example, when building RecipeCast’s Chrome extension authentication, I used the planning tool to identify “non-blocking” UI components. While I wrestled with the backend logic, I had an agent build out the isolated UI pieces in a parallel track.
...
1. Database schema changes (must happen first, can test
...
in isolation)
...
2. Backend token generation (
...
, can test API in Postman)
...
configuration (can verify with curl before touching
...
4. Extension OAuth flow (can test with
...
coded tokens before real backend)
...
. Integration testing (everything wired together)
...
I commit`.cursor/plans/` to my personal projects. These aren’t throwaway notes; they are markdown documents that stay in the repo to provide context across sessions and help inform documentation changes later.
...
When I’m ready to execute, I reference the plan file directly in Composer: “Execute task 2.2 from`.cursor/plans/external-auth.md`.” Once a task is verified, I have the agent update the markdown file to mark it complete. This makes it incredibly easy to pick up a project after a few days away.
...
## What This Actually Looks Like
...
Create a plan for implementing external client authentication. Think about sequencing so I can test deliverables one by one, and clearly identify which tracks can be worked on in parallel to minimize downtime.
...
### Step 3: Execute sequentially and in parallel where appropriate
...
Execute task 2.1 from`.cursor/plans/external-auth.md`
...
It implements the token generation endpoint. I test it in Postman with mock data, verify the JWT is signed correctly, commit.
...
### Step 4: Update the plan
...
a task is tested
...
Update the plan to mark task 2.1 as complete.
...
### Step 5: Verification
...
Cursor tells me what to test based on my rules:
...
- Test token generation with valid user ID
- Test token refresh flow
- Verify CORS headers in browser devtools
- Test extension auth flow end-to-end
- Check token expiration handling
...
Before using AI this way, I would hesitate before touching infrastructure or backend services I wasn’t deeply familiar with. Using this “Plan-Audit-Execute” loop, I shipped RecipeCast’s auth system and Python endpoints to production despite it being my first time writing production-grade Python.

Title: Cursor AI Agent Mode Walkthrough: I Let It Build a Full SaaS App
URL: https://ainskills.com/cursor-ai-agent-mode-saas-walkthrough/
Published: 2026-06-01T11:31:50.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
Cursor AI Agent Mode is an experimental feature that gives Cursor’s AI full terminal and filesystem access so it can write, test, and iterate on code autonomously. I used it to build a complete subscription billing dashboard with FastAPI, SQLite, JWT auth, and Tailwind CSS in about three hours. It worked surprisingly well but hit real limits on debugging and edge cases.
...
built, and
...
decided to test it with
...
I chose a subscription billing dashboard. It had to include user signup and login with secure password
...
I created an empty folder called cursor-saas and opened it in Cursor. I switched to Agent Mode using the dropdown in the chat panel. Then I wrote my first prompt.
...
“Build a SaaS subscription management dashboard with Python FastAPI, SQLite, Jinja2 templates, and Tailwind CSS. I need user auth with JWT tokens, plan management, customer subscriptions, invoice tracking, a dashboard with MRR and churn metrics, and optional Stripe integration. Give me the full project structure.”
...
The agent started immediately. It created main.py, set up the FastAPI app skeleton, and defined all four database models (User, SubscriptionPlan, Customer, Invoice) using SQLAlchemy. It generated requirements.txt with all the dependencies. It created the templates directory and the static folder.
...
I started with authentication. “Add user registration, login, and logout with JWT tokens stored in cookies.” The agent created the password hashing utilities using passlib with bcrypt. It set up JWT creation and verification using python-jose. It generated the login, register, and logout routes. It created the login.html template with a clean form and the register.html template with name, email, and password fields.
...
Next I added plan management. “Let users create subscription plans with a name, description, price, and billing interval. Add a toggle to activate or deactivate plans.” The agent added the plans listing page, a create form with POST endpoint, and the toggle route.
...
Customer management came next. The agent added a customers page showing all subscribers with their assigned plan, status, and creation date. It created the form to add new customers with a dropdown to select a plan. It also added the cancellation endpoint. The interesting part was how the agent decided to generate an invoice automatically when a customer was created. I did not ask for that. It inferred the invoice generation from the domain model.
...
The dashboard was the most complex piece. I asked for MRR calculation, active subscriber count, total customers, and churn rate. The agent queried active customers, summed their plan prices in cents, and divided by 100 for dollar display.
...
Jinja2
...
was debugging loops.

Title: Building AI Agents with Cursor
URL: https://laxaar.com/blog/building-ai-agents-with-cursor-1748650000028
Published: 2026-05-31T19:28:32.000Z
Author: Laxaar
Highlights:
Building AI agents with Cursor is less about writing agent framework code and more about configuring Cursor's built-in agentic machinery to do real work. Cursor is an AI-native code editor built on VS Code, and its Agent mode gives the model the ability to read files, run terminal commands, search the codebase, and iterate on output, all inside your normal editor workflow. That's the agentic loop, surfaced as an editor feature rather than a Python script.
...
This tutorial covers what's actually useful: Agent mode configuration, connecting external tools via the Model Context Protocol (MCP), setting up background agents for long-running tasks, and the failure modes we've hit at Laxaar while building production workflows on top of Cursor.
...
- Configure Cursor Agent mode for your project
- Write a`.cursor/rules` file that shapes agent behavior
- Connect an MCP tool server to extend what the agent can do
- Run a multi-step agent task end-to-end
- Set up a background agent for long-running work
...
term for the agentic loop
...
observe results,
...
you manually approving each step
...
's on by default in the Composer panel (Cmd+I on Mac, Ctrl+I on Windows
...
`.cursor/rules`(or the legacy`.cursorrules` file) is where you tell the agent how to behave in your project. Think of it as a persistent system prompt that applies to every Composer session. This is one of the most underused features in Cursor: most developers leave it blank and then wonder why the agent makes inconsistent decisions.
...
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard for connecting LLMs to external tools. Cursor supports MCP natively, which means you can give the agent access to databases, APIs, file systems, and services beyond what the editor provides by default.
...
At Laxaar, we've used MCP to connect agents to internal APIs and data stores on client projects. The pattern is consistent: define the server in`mcp.json`, confirm the agent can call it in a test session, then rely on it in the real workflow.
...
Let's run a real agent task that uses multiple tools. Open Composer (Cmd+I) and switch to Agent mode if it isn't already selected. Enter this task:
...
: Background agents
...
Background agents in Cursor run tasks asynchronously while you keep working. They're useful for long-running operations: running a full test suite, performing a large-scale refactor, or processing a batch of files.
...
Background agents use the same model and tool access as foreground Composer sessions. The difference is that they don't block your editor. You can switch files, write code, and check back on the agent progress when it finishes.
...
